node_types.description: An individual receiving or providing therapy (patient or therapist).

properties.name: role
properties.description: Role of the person (e.g., patient, therapist)

properties.name: cultural_background
properties.description: Cultural or religious background, including Islamic alignment if relevant

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

node_types.description: A broad, pervasive theme or pattern of memories, emotions, cognitions, and bodily sensations regarding oneself and relationships, developed during childhood or adolescence and elaborated throughout life.

properties.name: name
properties.description: Name of the schema (e.g., Abandonment, Defectiveness)

properties.name: core_beliefs
properties.description: Core beliefs associated with the schema

properties.name: emotional_needs_unmet
properties.description: Core emotional needs unmet that led to schema

properties.name: severity
properties.description: Severity or pervasiveness of the schema

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

node_types.description: A grouping of early maladaptive schemas reflecting a common set of unmet emotional needs.

properties.name: core_emotional_needs
properties.description: Core emotional needs associated with the domain

node_types.description: A general pattern of responding to schema activation (surrender, avoidance, overcompensation).
A specific behavioral, cognitive, or affective strategy used to manage schema activation.

properties.name: coping_style
properties.description: Type of coping style this response exemplifies

node_types.description: A moment-to-moment emotional state and coping response (adaptive or maladaptive) that is currently active in the individual.

properties.name: mode_type
properties.description: Type of mode (e.g., Child, Maladaptive Coping, Dysfunctional Parent, Healthy Adult)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

node_types.description: A discrete meeting between therapist and patient, including assessment, intervention, and relationship processes.

properties.name: session_type
properties.description: Type of session (assessment, cognitive, experiential, behavioral, relationship-focused)

properties.name: session_notes
properties.description: Summary or notes from the session

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

node_types.description: A specific intervention or method used in schema therapy (e.g., imagery, cognitive restructuring, flash cards, mode dialogues).

properties.name: technique_type
properties.description: Type of technique (cognitive, experiential, behavioral, relationship)

properties.name: indications
properties.description: When or for whom the technique is indicated

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

node_types.description: An individualized, integrative formulation of the patient's schemas, coping styles, modes, origins, triggers, and treatment plan.

properties.name: treatment_goals
properties.description: Goals for therapy based on conceptualization

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

node_types.description: A structured plan outlining therapeutic goals, interventions, and sequencing for schema therapy.

properties.name: cultural_alignment
properties.description: Considerations for cultural or religious alignment (e.g., Islamic values)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

node_types.description: A core psychological or emotional need relevant to schema development and therapy (e.g., secure attachment, autonomy).

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person exhibits or is affected by a specific early maladaptive schema.

properties.name: severity
properties.description: Degree to which the schema is present in the person

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a schema is part of a broader schema domain.
Indicates that a schema is typically associated with a particular coping style.
Indicates that a coping style is manifested through specific coping responses.
Indicates that a person is currently or characteristically in a particular schema mode.

properties.name: frequency
properties.description: How often the mode is exhibited

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a schema mode is rooted in or driven by a specific early maladaptive schema.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person participated in a therapy session (as therapist or patient).

properties.name: role
properties.description: Role of the person in the session

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a specific therapeutic technique was used in a therapy session.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a therapy session resulted in or updated a case conceptualization.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a case conceptualization is used to develop a treatment plan.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a schema arises from a specific unmet emotional need.
Indicates that a therapeutic technique is used to address a specific schema.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a therapeutic technique is used to address a specific schema mode.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a treatment plan includes a specific therapeutic technique.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
Chapter 10: Schema Therapy for Narcissistic Personality Disorder

edge_types.description: Indicates that a treatment plan is aligned with the cultural or religious background of the person (e.g., Islamic values).

properties.name: alignment_notes
properties.description: Notes on how the plan is culturally or religiously aligned
